episode.title: Contributor Onboarding — Ep 3 — Work Orders and Definition of Done

content.summary_short: Work starts with goal, scope, acceptance, and DoD; work finishes with preview proof, test notes, rollback notes, and a short docs note.
content.summary_long: AeroVista replaces vague tasks with work orders and a Definition of Done checklist. That turns review from opinion into proof, and keeps rushed changes from becoming hidden maintenance debt.

transcript: In AeroVista, work starts with a work order, not with vibes. If a task does not define the goal, scope, out of scope, acceptance criteria, and Definition of Done, it is still discussion.
The work order template forces that clarity up front. It also points contributors at the source of truth, references, and the expected test plan before implementation begins.

Definition of Done is equally concrete. The change must match acceptance criteria, avoid breaking the build, include test notes, avoid security regressions, include a short docs note, and use the correct paths and naming.

For review quality, add the boring but important line: what would you check or revert if this behaves badly? A rollback note does not have to be dramatic. It can be as simple as the file changed, the setting touched, or the old copy to restore.
The baseline is works in preview, not just works on my machine. Review should be able to reproduce the result quickly and see why the change is complete.

Short supporting docs matter too: what changed, why, how to run or test, and any gotchas. If a task changes direction, the decision should be captured in the decisions log instead of living in memory.

slides.title: No vibes-only work
slides.body: If the task lacks goal, scope, out of scope, acceptance criteria, or DoD, it is not implementation-ready yet.
notes: Missing one field is enough to pause.
Discussion is not the same as a work order.

slides.title: Work order anatomy
slides.body: A complete work order points to the starting files, examples, acceptance criteria, and the test plan.
notes: Start here defines source of truth.
Test plan defines how completion is verified.

slides.title: Definition of Done
slides.body: Done means acceptance met, build okay, test notes written, docs updated, rollback path named, and no security regressions.
notes: Correct naming and paths are part of quality.
Optional screenshots or metrics help review move faster.
Rollback notes help reviewers understand blast radius.

slides.title: Validation and documentation
slides.body: Short notes and searchable decisions keep momentum from turning into ambiguity later.
notes: Document what changed, why, and how to verify it.
Use the decisions log for durable changes in direction.

slides.title: Review-ready handoff
slides.body: A good handoff lets the reviewer answer four questions fast: what changed, why, how was it checked, and what should be watched after merge.
notes: Name the exact preview URL or local path used.
Call out untested areas instead of hiding them.
If the work touches shared behavior, ask for a narrower review owner.

engage.prompt: A task is missing acceptance criteria. How should it be treated?

options.label: As discussion that needs clarification before implementation starts.
As implementation-ready because the contributor can infer the missing details.
As done once the contributor gets it working locally.
